abstract: Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, enabling the rapid quantitation of cellular components based on accurate mass and chromatographic retention time. Driven by the need for high-throughput screening and the availability of high-resolution mass spectrometers, there has been a significant shift toward methods with minimal or even no chromatographic separation. However, eliminating the liquid chromatography dimension poses a substantial risk for misannotating structurally related metabolites in complex cellular extracts. In this study, we demonstrate that even electrospray ionization—despite being considered a "soft" ionization technique—induces in-source fragmentation that yields byproducts identical in mass to other common metabolites. Notable examples include the conversion of nucleotide triphosphates into diphosphates and hexose phosphates into triose phosphates. By evaluating yeast intracellular extract, we identified over 20 instances where in-source fragments could be mistaken for endogenous metabolites. Our findings underscore that robust chromatographic separation remains essential for the accurate identification and quantitation of a broad range of cellular metabolites.
